does: This paper introduces two security features for ChatGPT designed to mitigate emerging threats. Lockdown Mode restricts model capabilities and disables certain plugins and features to reduce the attack surface, making it harder for adversaries to exploit the system for malicious purposes. Elevated Risk labels automatically flag conversations that show characteristics associated with prompt injection attempts or potential data exfiltration scenarios, alerting users and organizations to suspicious activity. Together, these mechanisms provide defensive tools that help organizations implement security policies for AI model deployment in sensitive contexts. The work reflects practical security considerations in deploying large language models at scale, addressing real vulnerabilities that have emerged as LLMs became widely accessible.
connects: This work builds on growing research into LLM security vulnerabilities, particularly prompt injection attacks and data extraction exploits that have been documented across multiple systems. It extends prior work on content filtering and safety mechanisms by adding runtime behavioral detection and capability restriction as complementary defense layers. The approach resonates with broader cybersecurity literature on defense-in-depth strategies and risk classification systems used in traditional software security. This responds to the increasing demand for enterprise-grade safeguards as organizations integrate AI systems into production workflows, addressing gaps identified in earlier safety research that focused primarily on harmful content generation rather than system compromise.
enables: These features establish building blocks for more sophisticated AI security architectures that organizations can adapt to domain-specific threat models. Lockdown Mode creates a framework for tiered capability levels in deployed models, potentially enabling future work on fine-grained permission systems and context-aware capability scaling. The Elevated Risk labeling system provides a foundation for developing more accurate anomaly detection models trained on real-world attack patterns. Together, they point toward security monitoring and governance systems for AI applications, spurring follow-up research on automated incident response, behavioral threat modeling specific to LLMs, and organizational policies that balance safety with usability in production AI systems.
